frontmatter.title: Time-Dependent Perturbation Theory and Selection Rules

children.value: When a system is exposed to a time-dependent perturbation, such as an oscillating electromagnetic field, it can make transitions between the stationary states of the unperturbed Hamiltonian.
Expanding the time-dependent state in the unperturbed eigenbasis and keeping the leading term gives a simple integral for the amplitude of going from an initial state

children.html: ∣i⟩|i\rangle∣i⟩
∣f⟩|f\rangle∣f⟩

children.value: For an oscillating field, the transition is large only when the photon energy matches the level spacing,

children.html: Ef−Ei=±ℏωE_f - E_i = \pm\hbar\omegaEf​−Ei​=±ℏω

children.value: The transition probability is proportional to

children.html: ∣μfi∣2|\mu_{fi}|^2∣μfi​∣2

children.value: . When this dipole matrix element vanishes by symmetry, the transition is forbidden. For hydrogen-like atoms this yields

Δml=0,±1\Delta m_l = 0, \pm 1Δml​=0,±1

children.value: Setup: stationary states and a time-dependent perturbation

children.label: Setup: stationary states and a time-dependent perturbation

children.value: We consider a Hamiltonian split into an exactly solvable part and a weak, time-dependent perturbation:

children.html: H^(t)=H^0+V^(t)\hat{H}(t) = \hat{H}_0 + \hat{V}(t)H^(t)=H^0​+V^(t)
H^0\hat{H}_0H^0​

children.label: Electric dipole selection rules for hydrogen-like orbitals
